race.name: Cape Rouleur
race.tagline: 600 km. 6,000 meters of climbing. Five stages through the Cape Winelands on closed roads with pro-level support. South Africa's most luxurious cycling experience.

vitals.date: Early March annually (leads into Cape Town Cycle Tour)
vitals.date_specific: 2026: DEFUNCT. HotChillee entered liquidation in August 2024. All events cancelled. No Cape Rouleur 2025 or 2026.
terrain_types: Paved roads — closed for the event
Mountain passes of the Western Cape
vitals.start_time: Varies by stage — typically early morning departures from Franschhoek
vitals.registration: DEFUNCT. HotChillee entered liquidation August 2024. Event no longer exists.
vitals.aid_stations: Full pro-level support: motorbike outriders, lead car with nutrition, mechanics' van with spare wheels, race doctor, broom wagon. Feed stations on each stage.
vitals.cutoff_time: Flexible — support vehicles ensure all riders complete each stage
distance_options: Full Cape Rouleur: ~600 km over 5 stages with ~6,000m climbing
Optional Cape Town Cycle Tour entry: additional 109 km on the final day
vitals.entry_fee: GBP 1,350+ (entry only). Travel packages from GBP 1,400+ per person.

climate.description: Early March in the Western Cape is the tail end of the South African summer. Expect warm, dry conditions: 25-32C (77-90F) with low humidity. The Cape Doctor — a strong south-easterly wind — can gust significantly in the afternoon, particularly on exposed coastal sections. Mornings are typically calm and clear, making early starts ideal. Rain is rare but not impossible. UV exposure is intense at this latitude — sunscreen and arm covers are non-negotiable.
challenges: The Cape Doctor wind: strong south-easterly gusts, especially in afternoon coastal sections
Intense UV exposure — South African sun at sea level in summer burns fast
Heat: sustained 30C+ on exposed wine country roads with limited shade
Cumulative fatigue across 5 stages in warm conditions demands careful hydration

terrain.primary: Paved — closed roads through wine country and coastal terrain
terrain.surface: South African paved roads — generally good quality on the event's selected routes. The course winds through the Cape Winelands on roads closed specifically for the event, past vineyards, through mountain passes, and along coastal sections. Road surface varies from smooth tarmac in town to slightly rougher rural sections, but nothing technical.
features: Closed roads throughout — full road closure for each stage
Wine country rolling terrain with short, punchy climbs
Western Cape mountain passes with sustained gradients
Coastal sections with potential crosswinds from the Cape Doctor

gravel_god_rating.tier_note: A premium, fully supported multi-stage cycling holiday in South Africa's Cape Winelands. Not a mass-participation event — limited to 200-300 riders with pro-level support. Cycling Weekly called it 'the best event of its kind in the world.' High experience, low accessibility due to price.
gravel_god_rating.score_note: experience=5 for the combination of closed roads, wine country scenery, pro-level support, and South African hospitality. adventure=4 for the Cape Winelands setting and multi-stage journey. race_quality=4 for the HotChillee professional infrastructure. community=4 for the intimate group size and shared luxury experience. length=4 for 600 km across 5 stages. field_depth=1 as this is a supported sportive, not competitive. value=2 and expenses=2 reflecting the GBP 1,350+ price point.

logistics.explanation: Cape Town International Airport (CPT) is well-served by international carriers, and Franschhoek is less than an hour's drive into the Winelands. But South Africa is a long-haul destination for European and North American riders — 11+ hours from London, 17+ from New York. The event itself handles logistics beautifully once you arrive: transfers, accommodation, daily stage support are all included or organized. The challenge is getting there.

length.explanation: 600 km over 5 stages averages 120 km per day with 1,200m of climbing — demanding but not extreme for a multi-stage event. The cumulative fatigue across 5 consecutive days of riding in warm conditions is the real challenge. Each stage is a solid day on the bike. The optional Cape Town Cycle Tour on day 6 adds another 109 km for those with legs remaining.

technicality.explanation: Closed roads. Paved surface. Motorbike outriders clearing the route ahead. This is the least technical cycling experience possible — by design. HotChillee events remove every obstacle between you and the riding. No navigation required, no traffic to manage, no surface hazards to negotiate. You pedal. Everything else is handled.

elevation.explanation: 6,000m of climbing across 5 stages — averaging 1,200m per day — is meaningful but not Alpine. The Western Cape terrain is characterized by short, punchy climbs through mountain passes and rolling wine country rather than sustained HC-category ascents. The climbs are honest and frequent, but no single stage will test your climbing to the limit. The cumulative effect across 5 days is where the elevation makes itself felt.

climate.explanation: Late-summer Cape conditions are generally favorable for cycling — warm, dry, clear mornings. But the Cape Doctor wind can be savage on exposed sections, and the South African sun at this latitude burns fast and hard. Five consecutive days of riding in 30C heat demands meticulous hydration and sun protection. The climate is manageable but not benign — it is cumulative heat, not dramatic weather events, that catches riders.

altitude.explanation: Sea level to modest mountain passes in the Western Cape. No altitude concerns whatsoever. The highest points on the route are well below 1,000m. Altitude is a non-factor in every respect.

adventure.explanation: Five stages through the Cape Winelands — Franschhoek, Stellenbosch, and the surrounding wine country — is a cycling holiday in one of the world's most beautiful regions. The scenery encompasses vineyards, mountain passes, coastal roads, and Cape Town's iconic backdrop. The multi-stage format creates a sense of journey that single-day events cannot replicate. The adventure is more luxury safari than raw wilderness, but it is genuine and beautiful.

prestige.explanation: Within the premium multi-stage sportive community, the Cape Rouleur is well-known. Cycling Weekly's editor called it 'the best event of its kind in the world.' But the field size is tiny (200-300 riders), the media coverage is limited, and the event is unknown to the broader cycling public. HotChillee has brand prestige in the UK and European sportive market, but this is a niche within a niche.

race_quality.explanation: This is where HotChillee excels. Closed roads. Motorbike outriders. Lead car with nutrition. Mechanics' van with spare wheels. Race doctor. Broom wagon. Professional photography. Daily briefings. Post-stage hospitality at the Franschhoek base. The organization is modeled on professional cycling — riders get the pro experience without the professional legs. For a multi-stage sportive, the quality is world-class.

experience.explanation: This is the Cape Rouleur's defining strength. Riding on closed roads through the Cape Winelands with 200 riders, motorbike outriders, and a lead car carrying your snacks is as close to being a professional cyclist as an amateur can get. The setting is staggering — vineyards, mountains, coastline — and the hospitality in Franschhoek is exceptional. Wine tastings, gourmet food, poolside afternoons between stages. This is not just cycling. It is cycling as a luxury experience.

community.explanation: The small field size (200-300 riders) creates genuine community. You ride with the same people for 5 days, share meals, share suffering, share wine. The HotChillee community is international — UK, European, and South African riders mixing in a relaxed, social environment. Returning riders form a loyal core. The atmosphere is more cycling holiday than competitive event, and the bonds formed across 5 stages are real.

field_depth.explanation: Not a competitive event in any meaningful sense. No timing, no ranking, no categories. The field is composed of affluent cycling enthusiasts seeking a premium experience, not competitive athletes seeking a result. This is by design — the Cape Rouleur is about the experience, not the race.

value.explanation: GBP 1,350+ is the entry-only price. Travel packages through official partners start at GBP 1,400 per person. Add flights to Cape Town (GBP 500-1,200 from Europe), additional accommodation, meals, and incidentals, and the total cost approaches GBP 3,000-4,000. You get extraordinary quality for the price — closed roads, pro support, premium hospitality — but the absolute cost puts this event in the luxury category.

expenses.explanation: Long-haul flights to Cape Town dominate the budget for international riders. GBP 500-1,200 for flights, plus GBP 1,350+ entry, plus accommodation and meals not included in the entry fee. South Africa offers favorable exchange rates for British and European riders, making local costs relatively affordable, but the total package cost (GBP 3,000-4,000) positions this firmly as a premium cycling holiday.

biased_opinion.summary:
The Cape Rouleur is what happens when you give 200 amateur cyclists the professional treatment in one of the world's most beautiful cycling destinations. HotChillee's formula is proven: closed roads, motorbike outriders, lead car nutrition, mechanics with spare wheels, a race doctor, and a broom wagon. You don't think about traffic, navigation, or mechanical issues. You just ride.

The setting makes it sing. Five stages through the Cape Winelands — Franschhoek, Stellenbosch, and the surrounding wine country — deliver vineyards, mountain passes, and coastal roads in succession. The hospitality in Franschhoek between stages is exceptional. Wine tastings, gourmet South African food, poolside recovery. This is cycling as luxury experience.

The price reflects the quality: GBP 1,350+ entry is steep, and flights to Cape Town add another GBP 500-1,200. But for the cycling tourist who wants five days of world-class riding with zero logistics burden, there is nothing better.
strengths: Closed roads with full pro-level support — motorbike outriders, lead car, mechanics, race doctor
Cape Winelands setting: vineyards, mountain passes, coastline — five days of extraordinary scenery
Premium hospitality between stages: wine tastings, gourmet food, Franschhoek luxury
Leads into the Cape Town Cycle Tour — optional 109 km bonus ride with 30,000 riders
weaknesses: GBP 1,350+ entry plus long-haul flights — total cost GBP 3,000-4,000 makes this inaccessible to most
Not competitive: no timing, no ranking — purely an experience-based event
Small field (200-300 riders) means limited availability and no group drafting dynamics
DEFUNCT: HotChillee entered liquidation August 2024 — event no longer exists
biased_opinion.bottom_line: Five stages. Closed roads. Wine country. Pro support. If you can afford it, this is the finest cycling holiday on Earth.

final_verdict.one_liner: 600 km through the Cape Winelands on closed roads with pro support. Cycling's most luxurious sportive experience.
final_verdict.should_you_race: Yes if you want a premium multi-stage cycling holiday with world-class support in a stunning setting. Yes if the combination of serious daily riding and Franschhoek wine country hospitality appeals to your inner cycling tourist. Skip if the GBP 3,000-4,000 total cost is prohibitive. Skip if you want a competitive event with timing and rankings.
final_verdict.alternatives: If you want multi-stage sportive in Europe: HotChillee's London-Paris or Alpine Challenge. If you want South African cycling at lower cost: Cape Town Cycle Tour (109 km, single day). If you want wine country cycling in Europe: L'Eroica in Tuscany.

logistics.airport: Cape Town International (CPT) — 45 minutes to Franschhoek. Well-served by international carriers: direct flights from London (11 hours), connecting flights from Europe and North America.
logistics.lodging_strategy: The Cape Rouleur is based in Franschhoek — one of South Africa's most charming wine towns. Event accommodation is arranged through HotChillee and travel partners. Franschhoek offers luxury guesthouses, wine estate lodges, and boutique hotels. The town is small and walkable. Book through the event's official channels for the full integrated experience.
logistics.food: Franschhoek is the food and wine capital of South Africa. World-class restaurants, wine estates with tasting rooms, Cape Malay cuisine. Post-stage dining is a highlight of the event — expect braai (South African barbecue), local wines, and Cape cuisine at its best. The event includes organized meals and wine experiences.
logistics.packet_pickup: Registration and briefing at the event HQ in Franschhoek on arrival day. Kit and event materials distributed. Daily stage briefings each morning.
logistics.parking: Handled by event logistics — riders are transported to and from stage starts as needed. Transfers from Cape Town airport are available through travel partners.
logistics.official_site: https://www.hotchillee.com/events/cape-rouleur/ (DEFUNCT — HotChillee entered liquidation August 2024, website compromised)

history.origin_story: HotChillee, the events company behind London-Paris and The Alpine Challenge, launched the Cape Rouleur in 2013 as a multi-stage sportive in South Africa's Western Cape. The concept: bring the HotChillee formula — closed roads, pro-level support, premium hospitality — to one of the world's most beautiful cycling destinations. A 2012 test event with former Tour de France winner Stephen Roche, commentator Phil Liggett, and Cape Epic winner Karl Platt validated the concept. The inaugural public event in 2013 covered 475 km over three stages. The event expanded to five stages and ~600 km. HotChillee entered liquidation in August 2024, cancelling all events including the Cape Rouleur.
notable_moments: 2012: Test event with Stephen Roche, Phil Liggett, and Karl Platt validates the concept
2013: Inaugural public Cape Rouleur — 475 km over three stages from Franschhoek
Event expands to 4-5 stages, covering ~600 km through the Cape Winelands
Cycling Weekly editor calls it 'the best event of its kind in the world'
2024: HotChillee enters liquidation in August — all events cancelled permanently
history.reputation: Was the world's most luxurious multi-stage cycling experience before HotChillee's liquidation in 2024. May return under new ownership.

course_description.character: The Cape Rouleur unfolds across five stages radiating from Franschhoek into the Western Cape's finest cycling terrain. Each stage delivers a different character: wine country rolling roads through Stellenbosch, mountain pass climbs through the Hottentots Holland range, coastal sections with views of False Bay, and the dramatic landscapes around Paarl and Wellington. The riding is demanding — 120 km per day average with 1,200m climbing — but the closed roads and support infrastructure remove every variable except your legs and the scenery. The final day typically includes an approach into Cape Town, setting up the optional Cape Town Cycle Tour.

suffering_zones.label: Stage 1 — Wine Country Opening
suffering_zones.desc: The Cape Rouleur opens with a stage through the Franschhoek and Stellenbosch wine regions. Rolling terrain through vineyards, short punchy climbs, and the first taste of closed-road riding with pro support. The pace settles quickly as riders find their groups for the week ahead.
suffering_zones.terrain_detail: Rolling wine country: paved, short climbs, closed roads

suffering_zones.label: Queen Stage — Mountain Passes
suffering_zones.desc: The hardest stage typically features the Western Cape's most demanding mountain passes. Sustained climbing through the Hottentots Holland mountains or similar ranges, with gradients that test the legs after multiple prior stages. The scenery is spectacular — dramatic mountain passes with views across the entire Winelands region — but the climbing is honest.
suffering_zones.terrain_detail: Mountain passes: sustained gradients, 1,500m+ climbing in a single stage

suffering_zones.label: Coastal Stage — Cape Doctor Territory
suffering_zones.desc: The coastal stage introduces the Cape Doctor — the strong south-easterly wind that defines Cape Town cycling. Exposed sections along False Bay or the Atlantic coast can turn a flat road into a battle. The views are stunning but the wind is relentless. Group riding skills matter here.
suffering_zones.terrain_detail: Coastal roads: flat to rolling, exposed to south-easterly wind

suffering_zones.label: Final Stage — Into Cape Town
suffering_zones.desc: The final stage brings riders toward Cape Town, building anticipation for the optional Cape Town Cycle Tour the following day. This is typically the most celebratory stage — the group rides together, the support team relaxes, and the finish in or near Cape Town marks the culmination of five extraordinary days.
suffering_zones.terrain_detail: Mixed terrain, approach to Cape Town, celebratory atmosphere

course_description.signature_challenge: The queen stage mountain passes. After three prior stages of cumulative fatigue, the most demanding climbing of the week arrives. The Western Cape's mountain passes are not Alpine monsters, but at 30C with the Cape Doctor blowing and four days of riding in the legs, 1,500m of climbing in a single stage is absolutely honest.

guide_variables.race_terrain: Paved — closed roads through Cape Winelands and coastal terrain
guide_variables.race_weather: Late-summer Cape: 25-32C, dry, strong south-easterly winds (Cape Doctor)
race_challenges: 5 consecutive days of 120 km riding with 1,200m daily climbing in warm conditions
Cape Doctor wind on exposed coastal stages
Cumulative fatigue and heat management across the full week
Western Cape mountain passes on tired legs in the queen stage

non_negotiables.requirement: Multi-day block training — 5 consecutive days of 100+ km rides to simulate stage race fatigue
non_negotiables.why: The Cape Rouleur's challenge is cumulative. Day 1 is manageable. Day 5 on tired legs in the heat is where preparation shows. Your body must be adapted to the stress of consecutive daily riding at 120 km and 1,200m climbing.

non_negotiables.requirement: Heat acclimatization — riding in warm conditions (25C+) for extended periods
non_negotiables.why: Early March in the Western Cape delivers 25-32C on the bike. European and North American riders arriving from winter conditions will suffer in the heat without preparation. Heat training improves sweat rate, lowers core temperature, and reduces the performance impact of warm conditions.

non_negotiables.requirement: Wind riding practice — headwind and crosswind group riding skills
non_negotiables.why: The Cape Doctor is not a gentle breeze. Strong south-easterly gusts on exposed coastal stages can make or break a stage. Group riding skills — echelon formation, smooth rotation, crosswind positioning — are the difference between surviving the wind and being destroyed by it.

citations.title: Entries for first Cape Rouleur open — Road Cycling UK
HotChillee launches The Cape Rouleur in South Africa — BikeRadar
Cycling the Cape Rouleur, South Africa — Active Traveller

youtube_data.search_query: Cape Rouleur gran fondo cycling Franschhoek

videos.title: HotChillee Cape Rouleur 2020 Full Highlights
videos.description:
Your 6-day cycling journey in the Cape Winelands starts in the host town of franschhoek and finishes at the iconic cape town waterfront.

Franschhoek is situated just a 1 hour drive from Cape Town, with breath-taking scenery, warm hospitality, world class cuisine and fine wines making it the perfect host town.

Ease into your week of riding with a social spin followed by sundowners at Leopard’s Leap Wine Estate on the first day. Day 2 kicks off with an early coffee ride and then an afternoon exploring at your leisure. The day finishes with a sunset ITT Prologue where riders are seeded into one of three speed groups for the rest of the event.

On the three stages groups are fully supported with rolling road closures, motorbike outriders, lead cars, HotChillee Ride Captains, mechanical and medical support. All you have to worry about is turning your pedals and taking in some of the most stunning scenery in South Africa.

The stages range between 100 – 150km per day and you’ll be done by mid-afternoon. This leaves plenty of time for recovery by the pool, fine dining in the evening and sampling the world renown local wines. You’ll make lifelong friends both on and off the bike and, if you’re bringing a non-riding partner, there’s plenty for them to do while you’re riding and many African experiences you can share.

videos.title: Cape Rouleur 2019 - behind the scenes with Think Bike Marshals
videos.description:
Take a behind-the-scenes look at the Cape Rouleur, 2019 edition.

The Cape Rouleur is a 5-day cycle tour in the Western Cape Winelands and Overberg, ending in Cape Town.  After the first day’s individual time trial of 7 km, each day’s distance covered about 100-150 km.  (Correction to narration: traffic cops stop traffic; TB Marshals direct traffic flow or only stop traffic under cops' supervision).

Tour dates: 2019 March 3-7

- HotChillee Cape Rouleur: https://www.hotchillee.com/event/cape-rouleur/
- Think Bike: https://thinkbike.co.za/
- Velokhaya Life Cycling Academy: http://velokhaya.com/

videos.curation_reason: Provides detailed behind-the-scenes perspective of the multi-stage route through Western Cape Winelands with specific terrain and logistical insights.

quotes.text: It's a multi-stage cycle tour over five days through the Winelands of the Western Cape, the coastal roads and ending up in Cape Town. Almost all the way around the roads were in very good condition, though we did encounter some road works in progress.

Each day's distance covered about 100-150 km, with challenging intersections like the 44 and main road intersection at Somerset West where we just could not get this cleared before the riders came down that steep descent.

key_challenges.name: N44 and main road intersection at Somerset West
key_challenges.description: Very challenging busy intersection with steep approach that marshals could not clear before riders came down

key_challenges.name: Last big climb on Chapman's Peak
key_challenges.description: Described as the last big climb on day five, noted as a significant challenge

key_challenges.name: Franschhoek climb
key_challenges.description: About 800 meters of climbing described as tough, riders visibly tired with lower speeds at the end of stage two

terrain_notes.text: Almost all roads were in very good condition with only some road works in progress encountered

race_day_tips.text: Don't go near the gutter - avoid falling off and focus on getting back on track

additional_quotes.text: Everything was really well organized - each of the three cycle groups on the road had a lead vehicle to find the way, ride captain cycling in the group with them, six motorbike marshals to keep traffic safer, a medic, and a mechanics car
After a mechanical the rider gets brought back to the group by a ride captain and we try and keep the traffic safe around them

rider_intel.search_text: Cape Rouleur is a multi-stage cycling event over five days through the Western Cape Winelands and coastal roads, ending in Cape Town. The route includes challenging busy intersections like the N44 and main road intersection at Somerset West, where steep approaches make traffic management difficult. Key climbs include a tough 800-meter section at Franschhoek and the final big climb on Chapman's Peak on day five. The course travels through Stellenbosch, Gordon's Bay with views of the Indian Ocean, and finishes at the V&A Waterfront in Cape Town. Road conditions are generally very good with minimal road works. The event is well-organized with lead vehicles, ride captains, motorbike marshals for traffic safety, medics, and mechanics cars supporting riders throughout each stage.
